title: Syntagma
summary: Agentic curriculum lifecycle management system for PES University. Faculty submit raw course content, the agent refines it into structured records, the 36-tool editor lets admins propose reviewable edits, and every state is snapshotable with full diff history and official A4 PDF output.

images.alt: Agentic editor with chat, fields panel, and draft review
Single course PDF page with letterhead

metrics: Dual cache layer: Upstash Redis + in-memory with lazy invalidation
3 preview formats: HTML, diff, and A4 PDF with letterhead
Multi-download options: single course, semester, full curriculum
role: Designed and built the entire system: backend API, database schema, agent pipeline with 36 tools and type-checked tool registry, Jinja2 PDF templating engine, dual cache layer, CORS-proxied frontend on Vercel, and Docker backend on HF Spaces.
body:
PES University revamps its B.Tech curriculum nearly every academic year. Faculty submit course content in raw, inconsistent formats. Someone manually compiles it into the official syllabus document. No version history, no way to compare what changed between years. Entirely manual, error-prone, slow.

Syntagma replaces that with an agentic lifecycle management system. Faculty submit through a form, the agent refines raw content into structured curriculum-ready records (Pydantic-validated, course codes auto-parsed for semester/department/credits). An agentic editor with 36 type-registered tools lets admins propose and review changes through reviewable drafts that never auto-apply. The curriculum renders as official A4 PDFs through a Jinja2 templating engine with four template variants (single course, semester, full curriculum, program outcomes) and multiple download options (inline preview, attachment download, single course, semester-level, full document). Every change is tracked through a git-like versioning system: named snapshots, restore, and version-vs-version diff.

The architecture runs on a 12-table Supabase PostgreSQL schema with dual cache layer (Upstash Redis with in-memory fallback and lazy pattern-based invalidation), cache prewarm at startup, and Sentry error tracking. The frontend is vanilla HTML/CSS/JS served from Vercel with API proxied through to the FastAPI Docker backend on Hugging Face Spaces. CORS middleware handles the cross-origin bridge. The agent tool system is built as a frozen dataclass registry with typed JSON Schema parameters, enabling complex tool chains: the agent can chain batch course reads, spreadsheet generation, web research, draft creation, and version snapshots in a single session. From competitor research to drafting courses to generating complete syllabuses in minutes.

title: Raijin LOB
summary: C++ limit order book engine built around low-latency market microstructure ideas, benchmarked publicly and now moving toward a simulator phase.
metrics: Public benchmark brief and repository documentation
Phase-one engine complete enough to start simulator work
role: Designed and implemented the phase-one matching engine, documentation, and benchmark reporting path.
body:
Raijin is a fast limit order book engine built with consumer hardware in mind. The current public phase focuses on the core engine, benchmark reporting, and documentation around the design choices.

The project now moves into a simulator phase, where the goal is to model realistic market behaviour closely enough to support later RL and JEPA experiments.

The important part for the portfolio is the systems direction: market microstructure, latency-aware data structures, measured performance, and a path from engine work into agent simulation.

title: Project Halide
summary: Small-model diagnostic workbench for damaged film scans using MiniCPM-V, Nemotron-Mini, strict validation, overlays, telemetry, and a Gradio light-table UI.

images.alt: Damaged 35mm negative used to evaluate Project Halide

metrics: 7-9 validated defects on the public demo negative
79 edge artifacts filtered in the official Space run
GPU runtime validated on ZeroGPU A10G and T4 mirrors
role: Designed and built the full pipeline: model orchestration, schema validation, artifact filtering, overlay generation, history, UI, and evaluation notes.
body:
Project Halide is a diagnostic workbench for analog film scans. It does not try to magically restore the image. It extracts visible defect evidence, validates geometry, draws overlays, and writes a physical diagnosis that a human can inspect.

The important engineering choice is that the model is not treated as the final authority. MiniCPM-V proposes candidate defects, then a validator rejects malformed boxes, unsupported labels, edge artifacts, sprocket-lane noise, duplicates, and weak false positives before Nemotron-Mini writes the report.

The product lesson is simple: for messy real-world vision tasks, the validator and the UI are as important as the model. The interface exposes dropped boxes, edge artifacts, classical assist counts, raw JSON, and history so the user can judge uncertainty instead of being asked to trust a black box.

title: Cybersec OpenEnv
summary: Long-horizon cyber-defense POMDP for training and evaluating small LLM SOC agents with GRPO, realistic attack chains, and reward-hacking resistant containment incentives.

images.alt: Aggregate before and after GRPO performance across training and held-out cyber scenarios

metrics: +2.43 mean return versus heuristic on held-out cloud_metadata_ssrf
role: Built the environment, attacker scenarios, reward channels, GRPO training loop, evaluation protocol, and technical write-up.
body:
Cybersec OpenEnv asks whether a small local LLM can learn useful defensive behavior in a realistic long-horizon cyber environment. The setup models delayed alerts, partial observability, containment cost, adaptive attacker timing, and valid JSON actions.

The project became interesting when the model found reward exploits: isolate everything, or isolate the root asset at tick zero. The fix was not a bigger model. The fix was a better environment and an evidence-based containment reward that makes investigation before containment the winning strategy.

The held-out result matters most: the trained policy improved over the heuristic on a scenario it never saw during training. That suggests the model learned a transferable defensive pattern rather than memorizing attack graphs.

title: Breakout-DQN
summary: From-scratch Atari Breakout DQN study for building intuition around deep RL, preprocessing, replay, target networks, and early DeepMind-style agent design.
role: Implemented the learning loop to understand DQN mechanics from first principles.
body:
Breakout-DQN is a deliberate first-principles RL project. The point is not to beat every public benchmark. The point is to understand the mechanics behind the early Atari agents: frame preprocessing, action repeat, replay buffers, target networks, value updates, and the instability that appears when any piece is wrong.

The project belongs in the portfolio because it shows the learning path behind the bigger robotics and world-model ambitions.
